text: The surgeon frees the foreshortened mesentery, which is adherent to the small bowel and right colon mesentery, to better expose the transverse colon. The transverse colon is reflected upwards, revealing the duodenum.
The surgeon frees the foreshortened mesentery, which is adherent to the small bowel and right colon mesentery, by reflecting the transverse colon upwards to improve exposure.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and proceeds to dissect the ileocolic pedicle, entering the retroperitoneal plane over the duodenum.
The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, entering the retroperitoneal plane over the duodenum. The mesentery is carefully opened along the anticipated line of the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon opens the mesentery over the anticipated line of the superior mesenteric vessels and proceeds to thin out the ileocolic pedicle, which has been identified.
The surgeon thins out the ileocolic pedicle, identifying the ileocolic vein and dividing it to relieve tension. The ileocolic artery is then oscillated.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein and divides it, relieving tension in the area. Subsequently, the surgeon oscillates the ileocolic artery.

The surgeon works to unfold the foreshortened mesentery where the transverse colon adheres to the root of the small bowel mesentery, aiming to expose the transverse colon for proper margin selection. The tattoo marking is visible approximately six centimeters distal to the polyp, guiding the surgeon's transection plan. The mesentery is marked from the planned margin down to the middle colic vessels, which the surgeon begins to isolate.

The surgeon manipulates the foreshortened mesentery where the transverse colon adheres to the root of the small bowel mesentery, attempting to unfold it for better exposure. The tattoo marking is visible approximately six centimeters distal to the polyp. The surgeon marks the mesentery from the planned margin down to the middle colic vessels and proceeds to isolate these vessels.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node with black dye at the base of the middle colic vessels, thinning out the surrounding fatty tissue before electing to take the artery and vein together.
A lymph node with black dye is visible at the base of the middle colic vessels. The surgeon thins out the surrounding fatty tissue and elects to take the artery and vein together at this location.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the transverse colon mesentery, identifying marginal vessels along the bowel wall. The omentum is split at the planned transection level, and the omentum is taken to the specimen side of the colon. The surgeon enters the previously dissected medial plane and navigates around the hepatocolic ligament, noting some omentum adhered to the gallbladder.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the transverse colon mesentery, identifying the marginal vessels along the bowel wall. The medial dissection is finished, and the omentum is split at the planned transection level, with the omentum taken to the specimen side of the colon. The surgeon navigates around the hepatocolic ligament, encountering omentum adhered to the gallbladder.

The surgeon navigates around the flexure with the camera positioned in the left lower quadrant, initially struggling to obtain a clear view. The appendix is visible in the field. The surgeon switches to a sub-SQL view to join the previously dissected medial plane and proceeds to take the remaining lateral attachments of the cecum, focusing on the pedicolic ligament.

The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure with the camera positioned in the left lower quadrant, initially struggling to obtain a clear view. The appendix is visible in the field. The surgeon switches to a sub-SQL view to better join the previously dissected medial plane and proceeds to detach the remaining lateral attachments of the cecum, focusing on the pedicolic ligament.

The surgeon takes the small bowel margin and checks perfusion using ICG. A stapler is used on the colon side to ensure visibility of the ICG during stapling. The division of the small bowel occurs to the left of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon takes the small bowel margin and checks perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) visualization. The stapler is positioned on the colon side to staple while the ICG is visible. The division occurs to the left of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon uses a stapler from the suprapubic port to perform an isoparastaltic anastomosis to the patient's left of the falciform ligament. The stapler is positioned to create a secure connection, minimizing the need for additional port site closures. The common channel is closed starting from the inside to the outside at the near corner.

The surgeon uses a stapler from the suprapubic port to create an isoparastaltic anastomosis to the patient's left of the falciform ligament. The stapler is positioned to achieve a precise anastomosis without needing to close an additional twelve millimeter port site. The common channel is closed starting from the inside to the outside at the near corner.

The surgeon places a transition stitch to secure the anastomosis, lifting the far corner with the left hand. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is checked to confirm perfusion. The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to imbricate the top of the common channel back down.

The surgeon places a transition stitch to secure the anastomosis, lifting the far corner with the left hand. Indocyanine green (ICG) is checked for perfusion, showing satisfactory results. The surgeon uses a 3-0 nine-inch V-Loc suture to imbricate the top of the common channel back down, requiring only one suture for completion.
